--- SOURCE 3: Integrated metrology for advanced manufacturing - ScienceDirect.com ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197

SUMMARY:
For instance, metrology information from the manufacturing processes can be utilized to establish quality control loops in assembly, allowing the metrology data to permeate its original context and become a more significant part of the overall production process. Integrated metrology - Process flow of producing final product in advanced manufacturing (modified from [58]). The aim of this keynote paper is to elucidate the characteristics, benefits, and limitations of integrated metrology for advanced manufacturing, as well as the specifications required for the related i) measurement technologies, ii) data and communication methods, and iii) uncertainty and traceability through overviewing the progress of integrated metrology achieved in the past decades.

Integrated metrology for advanced manufacturing
===============================================

Author links open overlay panel Andreas Archenti(2)a, Wei Gao(1)b, Alkan Donmez(1)c, Enrico Savio(1)d, Naruhiro Irino(2)e

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cirp.2024.05.003](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cirp.2024.05.003 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0007850624001197&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

The transition from conventional standalone metrology to integrated metrology has been accelerating in [advanced manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/advanced-manufacturing "Learn more about advanced manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") over the past decade. This keynote paper defines the concept of integrated metrology, which extends beyond parts inspection and encompasses processes and manufacturing equipment to enhance efficiency and productivity. The paper presents the characteristics, benefits, constraints, and future possibilities of integrated metrology for parts, processes, and equipment. It also includes a classification of the physical quantities of [measurands](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/measurand "Learn more about measurands from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the corresponding measuring instruments, data and communication methods, uncertainty, and traceability. The paper also discusses future challenges and emerging trends.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001185)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001203)

Keywords
--------

Metrology

Integration

Advanced manufacturing

1. Introduction
---------------

Metrology is the science of measurement and its application [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0015)]. In the context of the manufacturing industry, its most common application is the verification of the product quality; that is the degree of adherence of a product characteristic to its specification [[97](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0097),[103](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0103),[104](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0104)]. The importance of metrology can be highlighted through the direct employment of around 100.000 people and a global market volume of approximately 30 Bn USD in 2019.[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#cit_1) Commonly, these applications are fulfilled by metrological equipment, which utilize various principles, including electrical, electro-magnetic, mechanical [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0020),[220](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0220),[242](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0242)], optical, laser, or X-ray based principles [[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0046)] via contact and non-contact modes of operation [[203](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0203),[238](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0238)]. The traditional metrology applications take place as stand-alone activities in the [manufacturing environment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/design-and-environment "Learn more about manufacturing environment from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for verification of process capability or product quality. As such, they require significant resources. Therefore, it is costly to verify all manufactured products, which may be termed 100 % inspection.

The European Metrology Networks (EMNs) define [advanced manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/advanced-manufacturing "Learn more about advanced manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as “branch of manufacturing that exploits evolving or emerging knowledge, technologies, methods and capabilities to make and/or provide new or substantially enhanced goods or services, or improve production efficiency or productivity, while ensuring environmental and societal sustainability” [[188](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0188)]. Advanced manufacturing is present across diverse application domains, spanning from aerospace, energy, automotive, electronics, medical, and optics. In all cases, the push to manufacture products more efficiently and productively comes from technological advancements linked to accuracy, cost and sustainability. Due to the challenges of stand-alone metrology mentioned earlier, research is carried out to improve the value and impact of metrology by bringing it closer to advanced manufacturing operations [[110](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0110)]. This new paradigm of metrology significantly contributes to the goals of high efficiency and productivity. [Quality control](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/quality-control "Learn more about Quality control from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") functions in manufacturing, enabled by metrology, can be categorized based on their level of metrology integration. These categories range from separate measurement rooms, which isolate metrology from production, to [integrated systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/integrated-system "Learn more about integrated systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") where manufacturing equipment and metrological equipment are the same piece of equipment, often referred to as a metrology system [[110](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0110)]. Ideally, metrology should provide accurate, timely, and reliable data with minimum latency to improve the performance of a manufacturing process. Metrology which is more ‘integrated’ has an advantage over the traditional stand-alone metrology. In addition to levels of metrology integration, quality control functions can also vary in terms of the extent of their applications. While single-stage quality control loops are common, multi-stage quality control loops are also possible. For instance, metrology information from the manufacturing processes can be utilized to establish quality control loops in assembly, allowing the metrology data to permeate its original context and become a more significant part of the overall production process. This approach is particularly valuable in industries where manufacturing precision is crucial [[234](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0234)]. In these cases, metrology data from the manufacturing processes can be used to individually pair components during selective assembly, ensuring the creation of fully functional products with reduced scrap rates and facilitating optimization in manufacturing across company borders.

### 1.1. Terminology and grouping

Gao et al. established the terminology in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#tbl0001) in 2019 [[58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0058)]. Their work intends to provide metrologists, engineers, and industrial practitioners with a vocabulary for an accurate communication. Gao et al. relate the terminology to a product, while this paper also covers the manufacturing process and manufacturing equipment (ME). However, to use information from the manufacturing equipment and the process to verify the quality of the product; the existing vocabulary needs to be expanded. This expansion relates to the variety of [measurands](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/measurand "Learn more about measurands from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), electro-magnetic, mechanical, dimensional, thermal, as well as the domains in which these measurands can be measured, processed, and interpreted.

Table 1. Measurement terminology and related measurands.

| **Domain** | **Terminology** | **Measurands** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Empty Cell | Empty Cell | Manufacturing Equipment (ME) | Process | Product |
| Environment | In-situ | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Ex-situ | Yes | No | Yes |
| Location | On-machine | No | Yes | Yes |
| Off-machine | No | No | Yes |
| In-line | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Off-line | Yes | No | Yes |
| Time | Pre-process | Yes | No | Yes |
| In-process | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Post-process | Yes | No | Yes |

The domain column in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#tbl0001) lists: i) The environment determines whether the measurement process takes place within the same environment as the manufacturing process (in-situ) or separately from it (ex-situ), with or without direct interaction or isolation. Examples for in-situ measurements are an infrared camera for the measurement of the workpiece temperature (Product) [[166](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0166)], measurement of the shape and location of a product during machining operation (Process) [[77](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0077)], measurement of the temperature of an extrusion nozzle for additive manufacturing (Process) [[120](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0120)] or measurement of the temperature of the spindle unit during the machining process (ME) [[159](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0159)]. ii) The location refers to where the measurement occurs in relation to the Manufacturing Equipment (ME) and a specific manufacturing step. Measurements can be within the working volume of the ME or attached to other components of the ME (on-machine) [[212](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0212)]. They can also be in [close proximity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/close-proximity "Learn more about close proximity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") to the ME (off-machine) [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0024)] or at another process step in the product's value stream, such as during manufacturing (in-line), or in a dedicated facility like a metrology room (off-line) [[229](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0229)]. iii) Time describes whether the measurement process takes place before the manufacturing process (pre-process) [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0008)], during the manufacturing process (in-process) [[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0054)], or after the manufacturing process (post-process) [[130](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0130),[133](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0133)].

### 1.2. Integrated metrology

Integrated metrology encompasses the above-mentioned types of metrology to obtain metrology data in multiple environments and locations, during multiple steps of the manufacturing operations, and sharing them throughout the enterprise for rapid and effective decision making. The term integrated metrology appears in some literature without a definition or a reference [[182](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0182)]. Although Bowen and Duncan provided a definition of it in the 1980s [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0022)], there is no commonly accepted definition of the term integrated metrology. Others derive definitions based on a common understanding across different groups such as industrial practitioners and metrologists [[132](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0132)].

In the context of lithography machines, integrated metrology has been recognized and defined since the 1990s [[199](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0199)]. As semiconductor devices grow more complex with smaller features and intricate structures, adaptive control based on integrated metrology becomes vital. The ‘integration' involves incorporating sensors, detectors, and measurement devices directly within lithography machines for real-time monitoring and control of parameters like alignment, focus, overlay accuracy, and dimensions.

From the authors’ points of view, this definition has to be further developed to broaden the understanding of the potential associated with integrated metrology, identify the limitations in the currently existing prominent case studies, and allow researchers to position their works. Thus, the authors propose a definition for the term **Integrated metrology**, as **_the incorporation and exploitation of metrologically traceable data within a_ _[manufacturing system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/gas-fuel-manufacture "Learn more about manufacturing system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_**_. [_[_84_](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0084)_,_[_124_](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0124)_,_[_161_](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0161)_,_[_187_](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0187)_]_

In this definition, the **_manufacturing system_** may include a piece of manufacturing equipment, a production process, a factory, or an enterprise.

The incorporation of metrologically traceable data means that the data is: i) freely accessible in raw or derived formats and timely available to all stakeholders at various degrees, ii) the information contained in the data may be of any type: a quantity value, an image, a time series of vibration data, but in any case it can be transformed into a quantitative value about the product quality, iii) contains so-called metadata that includes information about, for instance, the measuring instrument, test conditions, environment conditions, measurement uncertainty, measurement date and time, as well as other data that allow deriving information to correctly identify the product. In other words, these data are the relevant information to properly interpret and use the actual measurement results for the intended purposes.

[Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#fig0001) shows a non-exhaustive exemplary value stream of product manufacturing incorporating various metrology activities. Each production step on a manufacturing equipment (ME), for instance ME1, consists of a process which creates a product for the subsequent production step on the next ME. Each production step may employ varying degrees of integrated metrology. This can also be true for the value chain, for a factory or factories, or whole enterprises. Metadata, please see [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#sec0011), process data, ME capability and current conditions, and accuracy are exploited through integrated metrology, and incorporated through different production steps and organizational levels within the factory or the company. Metrological traceability, please see , of the data may be established through calibration using calibrated artifacts with an unbroken chain to the metrological artifacts of the National Metrological Institutes (NMIs) or the International Bureau of [Weights and Measures](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/units-of-measurement "Learn more about Weights and Measures from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (BIPM). Metrologically traceable data are integrated with production process information to ensure product quality and support quality assurance in subsequent stages. Accessible and timely metrology data facilitate informed decision-making, proactive planning, and continuous improvement.

![Image 11: Fig 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0007850624001197-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (339KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0007850624001197-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (339KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0007850624001197-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Integrated metrology - Process flow of producing final product in advanced manufacturing (modified from [[58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0058)]).

The aim of this keynote paper is to elucidate the characteristics, benefits, and limitations of integrated metrology for advanced manufacturing, as well as the specifications required for the related i) measurement technologies, ii) data and communication methods, and iii) uncertainty and traceability through overviewing the progress of integrated metrology achieved in the past decades. Challenges and trends for applications of integrated metrology in the next-generation advanced manufacturing systems will be identified.

In [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#sec0004), the focus is on the fundamental physical quantities of measurands that are closely linked to advanced manufacturing processes. These physical quantities represent critical parameters that must be precisely measured and controlled to ensure the quality of manufactured products. Various measurement instruments and techniques used to capture them accurately are discussed. Advanced manufacturing systems produce substantial volumes of data at each step of the manufacturing process. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#sec0011) delves into types/categories of integrated metrology data and the methods and tools for data acquisition and analysis. In [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#sec0016), the focus lies on addressing measurement uncertainty and traceability of measurements within the context of advanced manufacturing. When dealing with integrated metrology, it becomes crucial to first identify sources of errors and subsequently evaluate the comprehensive measurement uncertainty associated with the integrated metrology system. [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#sec0022) provides an overview of integrated metrology applications, categorizing them into three main groups: manufacturing equipment, manufacturing processes, and products. Finally, [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#sec0029) gathers the main conclusions of the paper in terms of integrated metrology and expected future trends in the field.

2. Measurands and measuring instruments for integrated metrology
----------------------------------------------------------------

A part or product is made from raw materials through a chain of manufacturing processes. For instance, in a mechanical [manufacturing system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/gas-fuel-manufacture "Learn more about manufacturing system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), the process chain is a combination of [manufacturing technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/manufacturing-engineering "Learn more about manufacturing technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") such as casting, forming, milling, drilling, turning, [grinding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/grinding-machining "Learn more about grinding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), polishing, chemical etching, and assembly [[235](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0235)]. In a [photolithography](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-lithography "Learn more about photolithography from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") system for manufacturing electronic computer chips, the process chain typically starts from crystal growth of a silicon ingot, followed by ingot slicing, wafer [chemical mechanical polishing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/chemical-mechanical-polishing "Learn more about chemical mechanical polishing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), material deposition, photoresist coating, exposure, developing and baking, etching and [ion implantation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ion-implantation "Learn more about ion implantation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ashing, chip separation, and packaging [[157](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0157)]. It should be noted that photolithography is composed of both mechanical and optical processes. For any type of manufacturing systems, measurement is an essential operation to cover the entire process chain. Measurement activities are applied not only to the final product for inspection purposes but also to each process for process control. In this section, the major physical quantities in manufacturing are classified and categorized as measurands in integrated metrology. Fundamentals and implementation of the corresponding measuring instruments for each group of the physical quantities are then presented.

### 2.1. Physical quantities of measurands related to advanced manufacturing

[Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#fig0002) shows the major physical quantities directly and indirectly linked to manufacturing, which can be categorized as electromagnetic, mechanical, and thermal quantities. The relationship between the quantities in and between each of the groups is also demonstrated in the figure. The [quantities thermodynamic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermodynamic-quantity "Learn more about quantities thermodynamic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") temperature (K), length (m), mass (kg), time (s), and electric current (A) are five of the seven base quantities/units of the International System of Units (SI) [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007850624001197#bib0020)].

![Image 12: Fig 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0007850624001197-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (563KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0007850624001197-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (563KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0007850624001197-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Physical quantities and associated SI units related to manufacturing (the quantities circled by solid line are related to more than one other quantity).

Others are the quantities derived from the SI base quantities. # Excessive precision compromises accuracy even with unlimited resources due to the trade-off in quantum metrology

[*npj Quantum Information*](/npjqi)
**volume 11**, Article number: 115 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

1129 Accesses

1 Altmetric

[Metrics details](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

This paper provides a unified definition of precision and accuracy from the perspective of distinguishing neighboring quantum states. We find that the conventional quantum Cramér–Rao bound underestimates the effect of statistical noise, because the biases of parameters were inappropriately ignored. Given that probability estimation is unbiased, defining precision based on probability distributions provides a more accurate approach. This leads to a correction of factor 2 to the traditional precision lower bound. The trade-off between precision and accuracy shows that precision can be further improved by sacrificing accuracy, while it should be restricted by the inherent precision limit determined by the number of samples. The inherent precision limit can reach the Heisenberg scaling even without entanglement resources, which, however, comes at the cost of significantly reduced accuracy. We show that increasing sampling may decrease accuracy when one pursues excessive precision, which indicates that the trade-off should be considered even with unlimited resources.

## Introduction

Accuracy and precision are frequently used to describe the quality of measurement and estimation results, but their meanings often exhibit subtle differences depending on the context. In parameter estimation theory, precision refers to the quality of the estimator, typically quantified by the uncertainty or standard deviation of the parameter. In contrast, accuracy denotes the deviation between the estimator and the true value, usually represented by bias[1](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR1 "Bevington, P. R., Robinson, D. K. & Bunce, G. Data reduction and error analysis for the physical sciences, 2nd ed. Am. J. Phys. 61, 766–767 (1993)."),[2](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR2 "Braunstein, S. L. & Caves, C. M. Statistical distance and the geometry of quantum states. Phys. Rev. Lett. 72, 3439–3443 (1994)."). It is widely accepted that maximum likelihood estimation can achieve asymptotic unbiasedness[3](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR3 "Fisher, R. A. Theory of statistical estimation. Math. Proc. Camb. Philos. Soc. 22, 700–725 (1925)."), meaning the bias approaches zero. Consequently, precision is often considered sufficient to fully characterize the measurement result, with its lower bound defined by the quantum Cramér–Rao bound (QCRB)[2](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR2 "Braunstein, S. L. & Caves, C. M. Statistical distance and the geometry of quantum states. Phys. Rev. Lett. 72, 3439–3443 (1994)."),[4](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR4 "Helstrom, C. W. Quantum detection and estimation theory. J. Stat. Phys. 1, 231–252 (1969)."),[5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR5 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Advances in quantum metrology. Nat. Photonics 5, 222–229 (2011)."), from which definitions of the standard quantum limit[5](#ref-CR5 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Advances in quantum metrology. Nat. Photonics 5, 222–229 (2011)."),[6](#ref-CR6 "Caves, C. M. Quantum-mechanical radiation-pressure fluctuations in an interferometer. Phys. Rev. Lett. 45, 75–79 (1980)."),[7](#ref-CR7 "Caves, C. M. Quantum-mechanical noise in an interferometer. Phys. Rev. D. 23, 1693–1708 (1981)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Yurke, B., McCall, S. L. & Klauder, J. R. SU(2) and SU(1,1) interferometers. Phys. Rev. A 33, 4033–4054 (1986)."),[9](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR9 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Quantum metrology. Phys. Rev. Lett. 96, 010401 (2006)."), the Heisenberg limit[5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR5 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Advances in quantum metrology. Nat. Photonics 5, 222–229 (2011)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "Yurke, B., McCall, S. L. & Klauder, J. R. SU(2) and SU(1,1) interferometers. Phys. Rev. A 33, 4033–4054 (1986)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Quantum metrology. Phys. Rev. Lett. 96, 010401 (2006)."),[10](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR10 "Holland, M. J. & Burnett, K. Interferometric detection of optical phase shifts at the Heisenberg limit. Phys. Rev. Lett. 71, 1355–1358 (1993)."), and the super-Heisenberg limit[11](#ref-CR11 "Beltrán, J. & Luis, A. Breaking the Heisenberg limit with inefficient detectors. Phys. Rev. A 72, 045801 (2005)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Roy, S. M. & Braunstein, S. L. Exponentially enhanced quantum metrology. Phys. Rev. Lett. 100, 220501 (2008)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Zwierz, M., Perez-Delgado, C. A. & Kok, P. General optimality of the Heisenberg limit for quantum metrology. Phys. Rev. Lett. 105, 180402 (2010)."),[14](#ref-CR14 "Hou, Z. et al. \"super-Heisenberg\" and Heisenberg scalings achieved simultaneously in the estimation of a rotating field. Phys. Rev. Lett. 126, 070503 (2021)."),[15](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR15 "Yang, J., Pang, S., del Campo, A. & Jordan, A. N. Super-Heisenberg scaling in Hamiltonian parameter estimation in the long-range Kitaev chain. Phys. Rev. Res. 4, 013133 (2022).") are derived. In certain practical sensing devices, precision is defined as the sensor’s inherent resolution or minimum scale. For example, in atomic clocks, precision is determined by the time interval between two “ticks”, which is fixed by the transition frequency of the system’s energy levels. Accuracy can also be defined as the ratio of precision to uncertainty[16](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR16 "Erker, P. et al. Autonomous quantum clocks: does thermodynamics limit our ability to measure time? Phys. Rev. X 7, 031022 (2017)."), essentially equivalent to the Allan variance[17](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR17 "Pearson, A. N. et al. Measuring the thermodynamic cost of timekeeping. Phys. Rev. X 11, 021029 (2021)."),[18](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR18 "Allan, D. W. Statistics of atomic frequency standards. Proc. IEEE 54, 221–230 (1966)."). At first glance, these definitions may appear quite different, with uncertainty playing distinct roles: in the former, it primarily defines precision, while in the latter, it quantifies accuracy. A key objective of this paper is to revisit the definitions of precision and accuracy and to shed light on the deep connection between these two concepts.

To unify these two definitions within a single framework, this paper addresses the issue from the perspective of probability distributions rather than parameter estimation. We define accuracy *α* as the degree to which neighboring quantum states can be distinguished, and precision *δ**φ* as the minimum detectable signal that satisfies the quantum state distinguishability condition at a specified level of accuracy. The previously discussed definitions of precision and accuracy are specific manifestations of this unified framework. Moreover, we identify a fundamental trade-off between precision and accuracy, governed by the intrinsic probabilistic and stochastic nature of quantum mechanics. This trade-off provides greater flexibility in the design of sensor scale (i.e., precision) for practical devices. For example, one can sacrifice some accuracy to achieve better precision, and vice versa. However, we also note that this approach does not always apply universally. In parameter estimation theory, there exists an irreducible inherent precision, determined by the number of sampling and the initial parameter. In some extreme cases, this inherent precision can reach the Heisenberg limit even without entanglement resources, but as a price, the accuracy even decreases as the number of sampling increases.

Defining precision and accuracy directly from the perspective of probability distributions offers two key advantages. First, unbiased parameter estimation is challenging under limited resources, as unbiasedness typically requires specialized unbiased estimators or asymptotically holds only for maximum likelihood estimation in the large *n*. This bias prevents uncertainty Δ*φ* from fully describing the quality of the measurement. In contrast, estimation of the probability distribution itself is always unbiased, and thus defining precision in terms of the distinguishability of probability distributions helps address this issue. We find that, for the same expected accuracy, a correction factor of 2 must be applied to the traditional precision lower bound. Second, quantum metrology can improve measurement outcomes by introducing additional measurement resources[19](#ref-CR19 "Bollinger, J. J., Itano, W. M., Wineland, D. J. & Heinzen, D. J. Optimal frequency measurements with maximally correlated states. Phys. Rev. A 54, R4649–R4652 (1996)."),[20](#ref-CR20 "Ockeloen, C. F., Schmied, R., Riedel, M. F. & Treutlein, P. Quantum metrology with a scanning probe atom interferometer. Phys. Rev. Lett. 111, 143001 (2013)."),[21](#ref-CR21 "Muessel, W., Strobel, H., Linnemann, D., Hume, D. B. & Oberthaler, M. K. Scalable spin squeezing for quantum-enhanced magnetometry with Bose-Einstein condensates. Phys. Rev. Lett. 113, 103004 (2014)."),[22](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR22 "Hosten, O., Krishnakumar, R., Engelsen, N. J. & Kasevich, M. A. Quantum phase magnification. Science 352, 1552–1555 (2016)."). From the perspective of parameter estimation, the role of all measurement resources appears to be the same and trivial, aimed at reducing noise to minimize statistical errors[5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR5 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Advances in quantum metrology. Nat. Photonics 5, 222–229 (2011)."),[7](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR7 "Caves, C. M. Quantum-mechanical noise in an interferometer. Phys. Rev. D. 23, 1693–1708 (1981)."). However, from the perspective of probability distributions, the mechanisms by which different resources enhance measurement outcomes are distinct. Only repeated sampling schemes effectively reduce statistical noise, while resources such as entanglement, multi-body product states, and nonlinear effects primarily serve to enhance the signal, rather than reduce the noise. These insights provide a clearer physical picture of quantum sensing.

## Results

### Precision and accuracy

From a physical intuition standpoint, the most straightforward definition of precision is the smallest detectable signal that a system can measure. For sensors with built-in scales, signals smaller than this scale cannot be measured, making the scale a direct indicator of precision. For sensors without built-in scales, there is more flexibility in defining precision. It is generally accepted that the minimum detectable signal should at least meet a unit signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)[23](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR23 "Degen, C. L., Reinhard, F. & Cappellaro, P. Quantum sensing. Rev. Mod. Phys. 89, 035002 (2017)."), otherwise, the signal becomes indistinguishable from the noise. Statistical noise can be quantified by the mean squared error:

When the sample size *n* is sufficiently large, maximum likelihood estimation can asymptotically achieve an unbiased parameter estimate, i.e., \({\rm{Bias}}[\hat{\varphi }]=0\)[3](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR3 "Fisher, R. A. Theory of statistical estimation. Math. Proc. Camb. Philos. Soc. 22, 700–725 (1925)."). Therefore, in parameter estimation theory, the uncertainty \(\Delta \varphi =\sqrt{{\rm{Var}}[\hat{\varphi }]}\) is commonly used to describe measurement precision. However, when resources are limited, it is not always possible to ensure that \({\rm{Bias}}[\hat{\varphi }]=0\), leading to potential inaccuracy in parameter estimation. Considering that in a binomial distribution, frequency-based probability estimation is unbiased, i.e., \(E(\hat{p})=E(k/n)=p\), we propose the following definitions from the perspective of probability distributions.

### Definition 1

For quantum sensors with built-in accuracy *α*, precision *δ**φ* is defined as

For quantum sensors with built-in precision *δ**φ*, accuracy *α* is defined as

Here, *φ*0 represents the initial known parameter, and *φ* is the unknown parameter to be measured, which are respectively encoded in the initial and final quantum states. For any type of quantum state, one can always perform a binary (yes-no) measurement, where the measurement operators consist of only two elements {*E*, *I* − *E*}. Therefore, \(p({\varphi }\_{0})={\rm{Tr}}[E{\rho }\_{{\varphi }\_{0}}]\), \(p(\varphi )={\rm{Tr}}[E{\rho }\_{\varphi }]\) represent the measurement probabilities for the initial and final states, respectively, under the same element *E*. They can also represent measurement outcomes projected onto the element *I* − *E*, but this makes no essential difference in the context of binary measurement.

In the subsequent sections, we will elucidate the rationale behind this definition by examining it from the perspectives of probability distributions and the distinguishability of neighboring quantum states.

For a weak signal, the quantum states to be distinguished are the initial state \(\left\vert \psi \right\rangle\) and the final state \(\left\vert {\psi }^{{\prime} }\right\rangle\) after interaction with the signal. We can select a suitable basis \(\{\left\vert 0\right\rangle ,\left\vert 1\right\rangle \}\) such that \(\left\vert \psi \right\rangle =(\left\vert 0\right\rangle +\left\vert 1\right\rangle )/\sqrt{2}\), and the final state becomes \(\left\vert {\psi }^{{\prime} }\right\rangle =(\left\vert 0\right\rangle +{e}^{i\varphi }\left\vert 1\right\rangle )/\sqrt{2}\). Using the known initial state as the measurement basis, these quantum states can be maximally distinguished[24](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR24 "Wootters, W. K. Statistical distance and Hilbert-space. Phys. Rev. D. 23, 357–362 (1981)."), some alternative proofs can also be found in the Methods section. The measurement outcomes are described by the equations:

Here, *F* represents the relative fidelity between the initial and final states, equating to the probability that one quantum state is measured based on the other. This process can be achieved through a Ramsey measurement[5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR5 "Giovannetti, V., Lloyd, S. & Maccone, L. Advances in quantum metrology. Nat. Photonics 5, 222–229 (2011)."),[23](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR23 "Degen, C. L., Reinhard, F. & Cappellaro, P. Quantum sensing. Rev. Mod. Phys. 89, 035002 (2017)."). According to Wotters’ criteria, two quantum states are considered distinguishable if the difference between their measurement results satisfies[24](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR24 "Wootters, W. K. Statistical distance and Hilbert-space. Phys. Rev. D. 23, 357–362 (1981)."):

This condition implies that the distinguishability of the quantum states depends on the measurement signal exceeding or matching the noise (i.e., SNR ≥1). For projection measurements, which conform to the binomial distribution, \(\Delta p=\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}\) for *n* independent samples. Substituting Eq.([4](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ4)) into Eq.([5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ5)) yields

defining the minimum distance or maximum relative fidelity between two distinguishable quantum states for a given finite resource *n*. Considering \(F=(1+\cos \varphi )/2\), the precision *δ**φ* can be defined as the minimum detectable signal that satisfies Eq. ([6](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ6)), that is

For large *n*, *φ**m**i**n* approximates to \(2/\sqrt{n}\), i.e., \(\delta \varphi \ge 2/\sqrt{n}\). The same concept can be directly applied to the parameter *φ*, that is \(\delta \varphi =\left\vert \varphi -{\varphi }\_{0}\right\vert \ge \Delta \varphi +\Delta {\varphi }\_{0}\). It is generally assumed that the initial parameter *φ*0 is completely known, so we have Δ*φ*0 = 0. This leads to the traditional QCRB \(\delta \varphi \ge 1/\sqrt{n{F}\_{Q}(\varphi )}=1/\sqrt{n}\). Notably, this differs from Eq. ([7](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ7)) by a factor of 2. We will explain the reason for this correction factor at the end of this section.

Indeed, SNR ≥1 is not an absolute criterion for distinguishing neighboring quantum states. As shown in Fig. [1](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Fig1)a, quantum states can still be partially discerned even when the SNR is below 1. While using this threshold to differentiate neighboring quantum states can enhance measurement precision, it may adversely affect distinguishability, thus compromising the credibility and accuracy of the results. Conversely, setting an SNR greater than 1 as the criterion improves accuracy but can reduce precision. This highlights the challenge of simultaneously improving precision and accuracy due to limited resources, driven by the fundamental inability to perfectly distinguish between two non-orthogonal quantum states. This challenge is rooted in the probabilistic and stochastic aspects of standard quantum mechanics, stemming from the superposition principle and the measurement postulate.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41534-025-01071-4/MediaObjects/41534_2025_1071_Fig1_HTML.png)

**a** The capability to distinguish neighboring quantum states varies with different SNRs. **b** Multiple quantum states independently probe the signal. **c** Repeated measurements enhance signal detection. Note that panels (**b**, **c**) serve similar purposes in parameter estimation. **d** Multiple quantum states are utilized as a product state to probe the signal. **e** A many-body entangled state is employed as the probe state. Notation: the “P” box represents the preparation of the initial state, the “E” box denotes the measurement and estimation of the final state, and the “R” box indicates the reset of the final state.

To quantitatively describe the precision and accuracy within a framework, we introduce a parameter *α* and modify Eq.([5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ5)) to account for this variability:

Additionally, Eqs.([6](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ6)) and ([7](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ7)) are adapted to incorporate *α* as follows:

Here, the range of *α* ∈ (0, +, *∞*). When *α* → *∞*, only two orthogonal quantum states can satisfy the distinguishability condition in Eq.([8](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ8)), setting the measurement precision to the lowest level (only the signal that drives the initial state to evolve into the orthogonal final state can be detected and recorded) while achieving the best accuracy (i.e., the two quantum states can be reliably distinguished). On the contrary, when *α* → 0, any two quantum states can satisfy the distinguishability condition. In this case, the precision can be very high, but the accuracy is greatly reduced, because even two identical quantum states can satisfy the condition, which is obviously impractical. When *α* = 1, the original Eq.([5](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ5)) condition is restored.

Eq.([9](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ9)) can also be reformulated as:

Here, the left side represents the signal = *δ**p* = 1 − *F*, disregarding the higher-order terms of *O*(*δ**φ*3), it can be deduced that \(\delta p=1-| \left\langle \psi | {\psi }^{{\prime} }\right\rangle {| }^{2}\approx {(\delta \varphi \bigtriangleup G)}^{2}\), which is also expressed by the Fubini-Study metric *δ**p* = *d**s*2/4 = *F**Q*(*φ*) ⋅ *δ**φ*2/4[25](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR25 "Anandan, J. & Aharonov, Y. Geometry of quantum evolution. Phys. Rev. Lett. 65, 1697–1700 (1990)."),[26](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR26 "Braunstein, S. L., Caves, C. M. & Milburn, G. J. Generalized uncertainty relations: theory, examples, and lorentz invariance. Ann. Phys. 247, 135–173 (1996)."). Here, *G* acts as the signal generator, such that \(\left\vert {\psi }^{{\prime} }\right\rangle ={e}^{i\varphi G}\left\vert \psi \right\rangle\). From this, we derive:

When *α*2 ≪ *n*, Eq.([12](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ12)) simplifies to:

indicating a trade-off between precision and accuracy. Notably, the right-hand side is proportional to \(\sqrt{n{F}\_{Q}(\varphi )}\), aligning with the conventional QCRB. Neglecting higher-order terms, we can explicitly define precision and accuracy directly from the perspective of parameters, such that

From this definition, the role of quantum Fisher information in enhancing both precision and accuracy becomes more intuitively evident.

Similar to the case in Eq.([7](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ7)), under the same accuracy expectation (e.g., *α* = 1), the QCRB as a precision lower bound requires a factor of 2 correction. This is because the parameter and the probability *p* are not simply linearly related, and the commonly used error propagation formula Δ*φ* = Δ*p* ⋅ ∣*d**φ*/*d**p*∣ does not strictly apply in this context. Thus the unbiased estimate of *p* does not naturally extend to an unbiased estimate of the parameter *φ*. In other words, because of the estimation bias of the parameter, using the QCRB as the minimum detectable signal actually underestimates the magnitude of the statistical noise, which is the origin of the factor of 2 correction. It is easy to verify that if the parameter *φ* and probability *p* satisfy a linear relationship, both *φ* and *p* become unbiased estimators, and the factor of 2 correction naturally vanishes. In fact, all parameter estimation is based on probability distributions, so defining precision directly from the probability distribution not only resolves the “bias” issue but is also conceptually more fundamental.

On the other hand, we can view traditional parameter estimation theory as equivalent to a quantum sensor with an inherent accuracy of *α* = 0.5 (which is, of course, lower than our ideal expectation). In this case, the expression for the precision lower bound returns to the traditional QCRB. Of course, in practical scale design, we can flexibly adjust the allocation between precision and accuracy. For instance, we could design a precision scale larger than the QCRB, thereby improving the accuracy *α* to meet our ideal expectation. For sensors with built-in precision, such as atomic clocks, the precision is fixed and represents the time interval between two ticks. The definition of accuracy *α* here is entirely consistent with that in the refs. [16](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR16 "Erker, P. et al. Autonomous quantum clocks: does thermodynamics limit our ability to measure time? Phys. Rev. X 7, 031022 (2017)."),[17](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR17 "Pearson, A. N. et al. Measuring the thermodynamic cost of timekeeping. Phys. Rev. X 11, 021029 (2021)."). In this case, increasing the quantum Fisher information or reducing the uncertainty does not affect the precision, but improves the accuracy. However, using a finer resolution (higher precision) with the same resources inevitably sacrifices accuracy. Similar trade-offs between precision and accuracy in time measurements have been identified in previous work[27](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR27 "Meier, F., Schwarzhans, E., Erker, P. & Huber, M. Fundamental accuracy-resolution trade-off for timekeeping devices. Phys. Rev. Lett. 131, 220201 (2023)."), attributed to the randomness of thermal processes. In contrast, the trade-off we identify here arises from quantum projection noise, stemming from the intrinsic randomness of quantum mechanics. Ultimately, the definitions of precision and accuracy can coexist seamlessly within our framework. The difference lies in one approach embeds precision, while the other embeds accuracy. Given limited resources, enhancing precision will inevitably reduce accuracy, and vice versa.

The above discussion can also be extended to POVM measurements {*E*0, *E*1, …*E**N*−1}. However, since the measurement outcomes now form a multinomial distribution, the quantum state distinguishability condition Eq.([8](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ8)) cannot be directly applied. An alternative quantum state distinguishability condition is as follows[24](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#ref-CR24 "Wootters, W. K. Statistical distance and Hilbert-space. Phys. Rev. D. 23, 357–362 (1981).")

where \({p}\_{i}={\rm{Tr}}[{E}\_{i}{\rho }\_{{\varphi }\_{0}}]\), \({p}\_{i}^{{\prime} }={\rm{Tr}}[{E}\_{i}{\rho }\_{\varphi }]\) denote the outcome distributions obtained from POVM measurements on the initial and final quantum states, respectively, and \({p}\_{i}^{{\prime} }\) encodes information about the parameter to be estimated. The key challenge is then to identify the optimal POVM measurement. A reasonable conjecture is that the optimal POVM should include the projection operator \({E}\_{0}=\left\vert {\varphi }\_{0}\right\rangle \left\langle {\varphi }\_{0}\right\vert\) of the initial state \(\left\vert {\varphi }\_{0}\right\rangle\), while the remaining POVM elements are orthogonal to *E*0. Such a POVM operator can completely eliminate the noise and uncertainty associated with the initial state measurement. The remaining task is then to identify the optimal POVM for measuring the final state. This POVM can be expressed as

For the initial state, it is clear that \({p}\_{0}={\rm{Tr}}[{E}\_{0}{\rho }\_{0}]=1,{p}\_{i\ne 0}={\rm{Tr}}[{E}\_{\perp }^{i}{\rho }\_{0}]=0\), and for the final state \({p}\_{0}^{{\prime} }={\rm{Tr}}[{E}\_{0}{\rho }\_{\varphi }]=F,{p}\_{i\ne 0}^{{\prime} }={\rm{Tr}}[{E}\_{\perp }^{i}{\rho }\_{\varphi }]={q}\_{i}\). Substituting these results into Eq.([16](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ16)), we obtain

Considering \(\mathop{\sum }\nolimits\_{i = 1}^{N-1}{q}\_{i}=1-F\), the formula can be simplified to *F*≤*n*/(*n* + *α*2), which is our main conclusion Eq.([9](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ9)). The corresponding trade-off relationship Eq.([13](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ13)) also naturally holds. Therefore, under a specific accuracy *α*, the obtained measurement precision *δ**φ* is also consistent with QCRB, that is, for a weak signal, Eq.([17](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ17)) is a set of optimal measurement basis sets. Similar to the binomial case, the probability of the multinomial distribution is also an unbiased estimator, but the parameter estimator itself is biased. Therefore, the factor of 2 correction is still required.

### The inherent precision in parameter estimation theory

In the Methods section, we demonstrate that the optimal measurement strategy is not unique. Beyond the method discussed in the previous section, which uses the initial state as the measurement basis, there are alternative measurement bases described in Eq. ([32](/articles/s41534-025-01071-4#Equ32)) that can also maximally distinguish two neighboring quantum states. Under these bases, the measurement precision is given by \(\delta \varphi \ge 2\alpha /\sqrt{n}\). This is consistent with the conclusions in the previous section, and thus exhibits the same trade-off relationship between precision and accuracy.

The trade-off relationship suggests that, in theory, precision can be infinitely improved at the expense of accuracy. However, in this section, we clarify that, much like actual sensors that have built-in scales or minimum precision, parameter estimation theory also implicitly contains an inherent minimum precision. In other words, with limited resources, regardless of how much accuracy is sacrificed, there will still be a minimum precision.

This inherent precision limit arises from the constraint on the number of sampling *n*. The measurement and estimation of parameters depend on the readout of probabilities, which are typically estimated from frequency and sample size *n*. Thus, with a finite number of sampling, there exists a minimum detectable probability signal, denoted by *δ**p* = 1/*n*. If the signal is too weak, it fails to induce a significant change in the probability distribution, making it impossible to extract meaningful information from the measurement. enriched_points.expanded_content: This study primarily focuses on precision metrology applications in advanced manufacturing, emphasizing integrated systems that enhance measurement efficiency and product quality.
Research findings may be influenced by measurement uncertainties and calibration complexities inherent in metrology systems, potentially skewing results and interpretations.
Limited access to high-quality metrology data can hinder comprehensive analysis, affecting the reliability of conclusions drawn regarding measurement efficacy in manufacturing processes.
